description: This figure presents SEM fractographs of tensile fracture surfaces from an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged (a-c) and hydrogen-charged (d-f) samples tested at 295 K and an initial strain rate of 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹. Panels (a) and (d) provide low-magnification overviews with 100 μm scale bars, while (b, c) and (e, f) are magnified images with 20 μm scale bars taken from the rectangular frames marked in (a) and (d), respectively. Both conditions exhibit fully ductile dimple fracture; however, the hydrogen-charged sample displays noticeably finer dimple sizes. The caption and text state that hydrogen refines dimple size from approximately 2–8 μm to 1.5–3.0 μm through void sheets produced by hydrogen-enhanced strain localization. Importantly, no grain boundary cracking is observed in the charged sample, indicating that hydrogen does not alter the ductile fracture mechanism. The figure supports the core conclusion that hydrogen-enhanced microbanding relaxes the internal stress fields of deformation bands, thereby suppressing grain boundary damage mechanisms and contributing to the high hydrogen embrittlement resistance of this steel.
